question: A heavy wooden crate rests on a smooth factory floor. Two workers pull it simultaneously using ropes attached along the same straight line. One worker pulls with a force of 250 N due east while the other pulls with an equal force of 250 N due west. Ignoring friction, what is the resultant force acting on the crate?

explanation.question_restate: The question asks you to determine the resultant of two equal forces acting along the same straight line but in opposite directions. You must consider both magnitude and direction before combining the vectors.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, let us understand what a force is.

A force is a push or a pull that can change the state of motion of an object. Force is measured in newtons (N) and is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

A beginner may ask, 'Why does direction matter?' Imagine two equally strong people pulling a rope from opposite ends. Although each person pulls with a large force, neither side wins because the pulls oppose one another.

The combined effect of all the forces acting on an object is called the resultant force (or net force).

When vectors act along the same straight line:

• If they act in the same direction, their magnitudes are added.

• If they act in opposite directions, one magnitude is subtracted from the other.

Mathematically:

Resultant = Larger force − Smaller force

If both forces are exactly equal, then:

Resultant = 250 N − 250 N = 0 N.

A resultant force of zero is called a balanced force. Balanced forces do not change the state of motion of an object. If the object is at rest, it remains at rest. If it is moving at constant velocity, it continues moving at that constant velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the forces.

Force 1 = 250 N due east.

Force 2 = 250 N due west.

Step 2: Compare their directions.

The forces act in opposite directions.

Step 3: Recall the rule.

For opposite collinear vectors:

Resultant = Larger force − Smaller force.

Step 4: Substitute the values.

Resultant = 250 N − 250 N = 0 N.

Step 5: Interpret the answer.

Since the resultant force is zero, neither force overcomes the other. The forces are balanced.
Step 6: Therefore, the correct answer is 0 N.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (250 N due east) assumes only the eastward pull acts and completely ignores the equally large westward pull.
Option C (250 N due west) makes the same mistake in the opposite direction by ignoring the eastward force.
Option D (500 N) is obtained by adding the magnitudes without considering that the forces act in opposite directions. Vectors cannot be added by magnitude alone when their directions differ.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently tests balanced and unbalanced forces. Before adding any vectors, always examine their directions.

• Same direction → Add.
• Opposite directions → Subtract.
• Equal and opposite → Resultant = 0 N (balanced forces).

Never add the magnitudes of opposite vectors directly.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two equally strong people playing tug-of-war. If both pull with exactly the same force in opposite directions, neither side moves the rope.

The two pulls cancel each other.

250 N east and 250 N west give a total (resultant) force of 0 N.

This means the forces are balanced.

The NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre also contains beginner-friendly lessons, worked JAMB examples, common examination mistakes, and additional practice questions.

question: A student is told that three forces acting on an object have been replaced by a single equivalent force. In vector addition, this single equivalent force is known as the

explanation.question_restate: The question asks you to identify the name given to a single force that produces exactly the same overall effect as several forces acting together.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, let us understand what happens when several forces act on the same object.

A force is a push or a pull acting on an object. Because force has both magnitude and direction, it is a vector quantity.

A beginner may ask, 'If several forces act together, do we always analyse each one separately?'

Not necessarily. In Physics, several forces can often be replaced by one equivalent force that produces exactly the same effect on the object. This equivalent force is called the resultant force.

The word 'resultant' simply means 'the final result obtained after combining all the vectors.' Instead of dealing with many forces individually, we replace them with one force that represents their combined effect.

Another beginner may ask, 'Does the resultant always have a larger magnitude than the individual forces?'

No. The magnitude of the resultant depends on the magnitudes and directions of all the vectors involved.

• If all forces act in the same direction, the resultant is larger.

• If some forces oppose one another, the resultant becomes smaller.

• If equal forces act in opposite directions, the resultant becomes zero.

Therefore, the resultant force is not simply the largest force. It is the single force that represents the combined effect of all the individual forces.
steps: Step 1: Read the important phrase in the question: 'a single equivalent force.'

Step 2: Recall the definition of a resultant force.

A resultant force is a single force that has the same effect as two or more forces acting together.
Step 3: Compare this definition with the options provided.
Step 4: Option C matches the definition exactly.
Step 5: Therefore, the correct answer is Resultant Force.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (Balanced force) is incorrect because balanced forces describe a situation in which the resultant force is zero. Balanced force is therefore a condition, not the name of the equivalent single force.
Option B (Component force) is incorrect because component forces are the individual forces into which a vector may be resolved. They are not the combined effect of several vectors.

Option D (Equilibrium force) is incorrect because an equilibrium force is a force equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to the resultant force. It is introduced to bring an object into equilibrium and is therefore different from the resultant itself.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently asks for the meanings of common vector terms. Do not confuse:

• Resultant force → the combined effect of all the forces.

• Component forces → the individual parts of a vector.

• Equilibrant (equilibrium force) → a force equal and opposite to the resultant.

Understanding these definitions will help you answer conceptual questions quickly without lengthy calculations.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine four friends pushing a car together. Instead of describing the push made by each friend separately, Physics allows us to replace all their pushes with one imaginary force that has exactly the same effect. That one force is called the resultant force.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Addition of Vectors and Resultant Vectors to learn how several vectors are combined into a single equivalent vector, understand common JAMB terminology, and practise additional worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and exam-focused practice.

question: A Physics teacher explains vector addition by saying, "To add two displacement vectors, place the tail of the second vector at the head of the first vector. The vector drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the second vector represents the final answer." What is the name of this method of adding vectors?

explanation.question_restate: The question describes a procedure for adding vectors without showing a diagram. You are required to identify the name of the vector addition method being described.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, let us understand why vectors cannot always be added like ordinary numbers.

A vector quantity has both magnitude and direction. Therefore, when adding vectors, we must consider not only their sizes but also the directions in which they act.

A beginner may ask, 'What do the words head and tail mean?'

A vector is usually represented by an arrow.

• The tail is the point where the arrow begins.
• The head is the pointed end of the arrow, showing the direction of the vector.

One of the simplest graphical methods of adding vectors is the head-to-tail method. In this method:

1. Draw the first vector.
2. Place the tail of the second vector at the head of the first vector.
3. Draw a new arrow from the tail of the first vector to the head of the second vector.

This new arrow represents the resultant vector.

A beginner may ask, 'Why does this method work?'

It works because it represents the actual path followed when one displacement occurs after another. The resultant connects the starting point directly to the final position.

Although this question describes the method in words, the same principle applies whether vectors represent force, displacement, or velocity.
steps: Step 1: Read the description carefully rather than looking for numbers to calculate.
Step 2: Notice the instruction: 'Place the tail of the second vector at the head of the first vector.'

Step 3: Recall the names of common vector methods.

The described procedure is known as the head-to-tail method of vector addition.
Step 4: The final vector drawn from the starting point to the finishing point is called the resultant vector.
Step 5: Therefore, the correct answer is Head-to-tail method.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (Vector resolution method) is incorrect because vector resolution is the process of splitting a single vector into components, not combining vectors.

Option C (Component method) is incorrect because component methods involve resolving vectors into horizontal and vertical components before combining them mathematically. That is not the procedure described in the question.
Option D (Cancellation method) is incorrect because there is no standard vector-addition technique in elementary Physics known as the cancellation method.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB sometimes describes a scientific procedure instead of mentioning its name directly. Learn to recognise the wording associated with common vector methods. Whenever you read 'place the tail of one vector at the head of another,' immediately think of the head-to-tail method used for vector addition.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine taking two journeys. First, you walk from your house to the market. Without returning home, you continue from the market to the bank. The second journey starts where the first one ends. That is exactly what the head-to-tail method does. The straight arrow from your house to the bank represents the resultant vector.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Addition of Vectors to learn graphical methods of vector addition, including the head-to-tail technique, triangle law, and parallelogram law. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, worked JAMB examples, and additional exam-focused practice.

question: A surveyor walks 5 km due east from a reference point to inspect a pipeline. He then walks 12 km due north to another inspection point. Which of the following correctly represents his resultant displacement from the reference point?

explanation.question_restate: The question requires you to determine the resultant displacement after two successive movements made at right angles to each other. Since displacement is a vector quantity, the final answer must include both its magnitude and its direction.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, let us understand the key ideas involved.

A beginner may ask, 'What is displacement?'

Displacement is the straight-line distance from the starting point to the final position together with the direction of that straight line. It is different from distance because distance measures the total path travelled, whereas displacement considers only the starting and ending positions.

Another beginner may ask, 'Why can't I simply add 5 km and 12 km to obtain 17 km?'

You can only add distances directly when the movements are along the same straight line and in the same direction. In this question, the surveyor first walks east and then north. East and north are perpendicular directions, meaning they meet at an angle of 90°.

When two displacement vectors are perpendicular, they form two sides of a right-angled triangle. The straight line joining the starting point to the final position is called the **resultant displacement**.

To calculate the magnitude of the resultant displacement, we use **Pythagoras' theorem**.

Pythagoras' theorem states:

Resultant² = (Horizontal displacement)² + (Vertical displacement)²

or simply:

R² = x² + y²

where:

• R is the resultant displacement.
• x is the horizontal displacement.
• y is the vertical displacement.

The direction of the resultant is between east and north, which is described as north-east.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the two displacement vectors.

Horizontal displacement = 5 km due east.

Vertical displacement = 12 km due north.

Step 2: Observe that the two directions are perpendicular.

Since east and north are 90° apart, the displacements form a right-angled triangle.

Step 3: Recall the appropriate formula.

R² = x² + y²

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

R² = 5² + 12²

= 25 + 144

= 169

Step 5: Take the square root of both sides.

R = √169

= 13 km.

Step 6: Determine the direction.

The final position is east of the starting point and also north of it. Therefore, the resultant displacement points towards the north-east.

Hence, the resultant displacement is **13 km in a north-east direction**.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (17 km due north) is obtained by simply adding the two distances (5 + 12). This ignores the fact that the movements occur in different directions. It also gives the wrong direction.
Option C (7 km in a north-east direction) results from subtracting the two displacements (12 − 5). Subtraction is inappropriate because the vectors are perpendicular rather than opposite.
Option D (12 km due east) is incorrect because it ignores the northward movement entirely. The surveyor's final position depends on both movements.
explanation.exam_tip: One of JAMB's favourite vector questions involves movement first towards the east or west and then towards the north or south. Whenever the two directions are perpendicular, immediately think of a right-angled triangle and apply Pythagoras' theorem. Also remember that displacement is incomplete unless both its magnitude and direction are stated.
explanation.final_answer: A. 13 km in a north-east direction
explanation.simple_explanation:
Suppose you stand at the starting point. First, you walk 5 km towards the east. Next, you turn and walk 12 km towards the north.

Instead of following the path you walked, imagine drawing a straight line directly from where you started to where you finished. That straight line is your displacement.

Because the two movements are at right angles, we use Pythagoras' theorem.

5² = 25

12² = 144

25 + 144 = 169

√169 = 13 km.

Since your final position is both east and north of the starting point, the direction is north-east.

Therefore, the resultant displacement is **13 km in a north-east direction**.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for more beginner-friendly lessons, worked examples, and exam-focused practice.

question: A mobile network field engineer walks 60 m due east from a service vehicle to inspect a signal mast. He then walks 80 m due north to reach the control panel. To determine his final position relative to the service vehicle, he uses the head-to-tail method of vector addition. Which statement correctly describes the head-to-tail method used in obtaining the resultant displacement?

options.A: The tail of the second vector is placed at the head of the first vector, and the resultant is drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the second vector.
options.B: The heads of both vectors are joined together, and the resultant is drawn between their tails.
options.C: The tails of both vectors are placed together, and the resultant is always equal to their arithmetic sum.
options.D: The second vector is reversed before joining it to the first vector, and the resultant is drawn between the two heads.

answer_text: The tail of the second vector is placed at the head of the first vector, and the resultant is drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the second vector.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the head-to-tail method of vector addition. Rather than calculating the magnitude of the resultant, you are required to identify the correct procedure for adding two vectors graphically.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, let us understand what a vector is.

A vector is a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Magnitude tells us how much of the quantity there is, while direction tells us where it acts or points. Examples include displacement, velocity, acceleration and force.

A beginner may ask, 'Why can't we simply add vectors like ordinary numbers?'

The reason is that vectors may point in different directions. Two vectors of equal magnitude can produce different results depending on the angle between them. Therefore, both magnitude and direction must be considered during addition.

One graphical method of adding vectors is called the head-to-tail method (also called the tip-to-tail method).

A beginner may then ask, 'What do
and

mean?'

Every vector is represented by an arrow.

• The tail is the point where the arrow starts.
• The head (or tip) is the pointed end of the arrow indicating the direction.

In the head-to-tail method:

1. Draw the first vector to scale in its correct direction.
2. Place the tail of the second vector exactly at the head of the first vector.
3. Draw the second vector to the same scale.
4. The resultant vector is drawn from the tail of the first vector directly to the head of the last vector.

The resultant vector represents the combined effect of all the vectors added together.

This method works for two vectors as well as several vectors arranged consecutively.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what the engineer is doing.

The engineer first walks 60 m due east and then 80 m due north. These two movements are displacement vectors because each has both magnitude and direction.

Step 2: Recall the rule for the head-to-tail method.

After drawing the first vector, the second vector must begin where the first one ends. In other words, the tail of the second vector is placed at the head of the first vector.

Step 3: Determine how the resultant is drawn.

The resultant does not start from the second vector. Instead, it starts from the very beginning (the tail of the first vector) and ends at the final position (the head of the second vector).

Step 4: Compare this procedure with the options provided.

Only Option A correctly describes both the placement of the vectors and the correct way of drawing the resultant vector.
Step 5: Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because simply joining the heads of two vectors does not represent vector addition. The head-to-tail method requires the second vector to begin where the first vector ends.

Option C is incorrect because placing both tails together describes the starting arrangement for the parallelogram law of vector addition, not the head-to-tail method. Furthermore, the resultant is not always equal to the ordinary arithmetic sum because direction must be considered.

Option D is incorrect because reversing the second vector changes its direction and therefore changes the physical situation. Reversing a vector is associated with vector subtraction, not vector addition.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently tests your understanding of graphical vector addition by asking about the correct construction rather than requiring lengthy calculations.

Remember these key ideas:

• Head-to-tail method → Tail of the second vector joins the head of the first.
• Resultant → Drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the final vector.
• Never reverse a vector unless you are performing vector subtraction.

A quick memory aid is:

'Join Head to Tail, then draw Start to Finish.'
explanation.final_answer: A. The tail of the second vector is placed at the head of the first vector, and the resultant is drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the second vector.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine walking from your house to a shop and then from the shop to a friend's house.

Your first journey ends at the shop. Your second journey begins from the shop. This is exactly how the head-to-tail method works.

The first vector ends where the second one begins.

After drawing both movements, you simply draw one straight arrow from where you started to where you finally ended. That single arrow is called the resultant vector because it represents your overall movement.

Therefore, Option A correctly explains the head-to-tail method.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, interactive illustrations and additional practice questions.

question: A mobile network field engineer needs to move communication equipment across a construction site. A scale drawing is prepared to represent two successive movements using the graphical head-to-tail method. The first movement is represented by an arrow 4 cm long due east, while the second movement is represented by an arrow 3 cm long due north starting from the head of the first arrow. If the chosen scale is 1 cm represents 10 m, what is the actual magnitude of the engineer's resultant displacement?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of graphical vector addition using the head-to-tail method. You are required to interpret a scale drawing, determine the length of the resultant vector, and convert it into the actual distance represented by the chosen scale.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, let us understand what graphical vector addition means.

A vector is a physical quantity that has both magnitude (size) and direction. Examples include displacement, velocity and force.

When two or more vectors act one after another, they can be combined into a single vector called the **resultant vector**. The resultant vector produces the same overall effect as all the individual vectors acting together.

One common method of adding vectors graphically is the **head-to-tail method**.

A beginner may ask, 'Why is it called the head-to-tail method?'

Every vector is drawn as an arrow.

• The tail is where the arrow begins.
• The head is the pointed end showing the direction.

To add vectors graphically, the tail of the second vector is placed exactly at the head of the first vector. If there are more vectors, the process continues in the same way.

After all vectors have been drawn, the resultant vector is obtained by drawing a single arrow from the tail of the first vector to the head of the last vector.

Another beginner may ask, 'Why do we use a scale?'

In real life, vectors may represent distances of several metres or kilometres. It is not practical to draw such large distances on paper. Therefore, we choose a convenient scale.

For this question:

1 cm on the drawing = 10 m in reality.

This means every centimetre measured on the paper represents 10 metres on the ground.

The two given movements are perpendicular because one is due east while the other is due north. Whenever two vectors are at right angles (90°), the length of the resultant can be obtained using **Pythagoras' theorem**.

Pythagoras' theorem states:

Resultant² = Horizontal² + Vertical²

This formula comes from the geometry of a right-angled triangle and is valid only when the two vectors are perpendicular.

Related formulas that are useful in this topic include:

• Resultant = √(Horizontal² + Vertical²) for perpendicular vectors.
• Scale conversion = Measured drawing length × Scale value.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the lengths shown on the scale drawing.

Horizontal vector = 4 cm due east.

Vertical vector = 3 cm due north.
Step 2: Recognize that the two vectors are perpendicular because east and north are directions that form an angle of 90°.

Step 3: Apply Pythagoras' theorem to determine the length of the resultant on the drawing.

Resultant² = 4² + 3²

= 16 + 9

= 25

Step 4: Take the square root of both sides.

Resultant = √25 = 5 cm.

This means the resultant vector measures 5 cm on the drawing.

Step 5: Convert the drawing measurement to the actual displacement.

Given scale:

1 cm = 10 m

Therefore,

5 cm = 5 × 10 m = 50 m.

Step 6: Compare your answer with the available options.

The correct answer is **50 m**.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (60 m) is incorrect. This would correspond to a resultant drawing length of 6 cm. Some candidates obtain this by adding 4 cm and 3 cm incorrectly before making another arithmetic error during conversion. Vectors at right angles are not added by simple arithmetic.

Option C (70 m) is incorrect. This comes from adding the two drawing lengths directly (4 cm + 3 cm = 7 cm) and converting 7 cm into 70 m. This ignores the fact that the vectors act at 90°, so the resultant must be found using Pythagoras' theorem.

Option D (80 m) is incorrect. This value does not correspond to the correct graphical construction. It may arise from misreading the scale or using an incorrect drawing length. Always measure or calculate the resultant first before applying the scale.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently tests graphical vector addition in two stages:

• First, determine the length of the resultant vector on the scale drawing.
• Second, convert the measured length using the given scale.

Do not convert each vector into actual units before determining the graphical resultant unless the question specifically requires an analytical solution. Also remember that vectors acting at right angles should not be added arithmetically; they are combined using Pythagoras' theorem.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine walking 4 cm to the right on a map and then 3 cm upward. Your final position is not 7 cm away because the two movements are at right angles.

Instead, they form a right-angled triangle.

Using Pythagoras' theorem:

4² + 3² = 16 + 9 = 25

√25 = 5 cm.

Since every 1 cm represents 10 m, the real displacement is:

5 × 10 = 50 m.

Therefore, the engineer's resultant displacement is **50 m**.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed beginner-friendly lessons, animated illustrations, and additional practice questions.

question: A road construction engineer walks 24 m due east from a survey point to inspect a drainage channel. He then walks 18 m due north to examine a road embankment. What is the magnitude of his resultant displacement from the survey point?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the resultant displacement produced when two successive displacements act at right angles to each other. You must identify the appropriate method of vector addition and calculate the magnitude of the resultant vector.
explanation.principle:
To solve this question, we must first understand what **displacement** means.

A beginner may ask, 'What is the difference between distance and displacement?'

Distance is the total length of the path travelled. It is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only.

Displacement is the straight-line change in position from the starting point to the final position. It is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is a resultant vector?'

When two or more vectors act together, they can be replaced by one single vector that produces exactly the same overall effect. This single vector is called the **resultant vector**.

In this question, the engineer first walks east and then north. Since east and north are perpendicular directions, the two displacement vectors form a right-angled triangle.

Whenever two vectors are perpendicular, the magnitude of the resultant is calculated using **Pythagoras' theorem**.

Before using the formula, let us understand where it comes from.

Pythagoras' theorem is a mathematical relationship that applies to every right-angled triangle. It states that the square of the longest side (called the hypotenuse) is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides.

In vector addition:

Resultant² = Horizontal Component² + Vertical Component²

or

R² = x² + y²

where:

• R is the magnitude of the resultant vector.
• x is the horizontal vector.
• y is the vertical vector.

After finding R², take the square root to obtain the magnitude of the resultant.

This formula is valid only when the two vectors are at an angle of 90°.

Related formulas you should know include:

• Resultant = Sum of vectors (same direction).
• Resultant = Difference of vectors (opposite directions).
• Resultant = √(x² + y²) (perpendicular vectors).
steps:
Step 1: Identify the two displacement vectors.

Horizontal displacement = 24 m due east.

Vertical displacement = 18 m due north.

Step 2: Observe that east and north are perpendicular directions, meaning the vectors meet at an angle of 90°.

Therefore, Pythagoras' theorem can be used.

Step 3: Write the formula.

R² = x² + y²

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

R² = 24² + 18²

Step 5: Square each value separately.

24² = 576

18² = 324

Step 6: Add the squares.

R² = 576 + 324 = 900

Step 7: Take the square root of both sides.

R = √900 = 30 m.

Step 8: Compare the calculated value with the available options.

Therefore, the magnitude of the resultant displacement is **30 m**.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (36 m) is incorrect. This value may result from an incorrect calculation or from applying Pythagoras' theorem improperly. Always square the individual vectors before adding them.

Option C (42 m) is incorrect. This is obtained by simply adding the two displacements (24 + 18). That method is only correct when both vectors act in exactly the same direction. East and north are perpendicular directions, so simple addition is not appropriate.

Option D (54 m) is incorrect. This value exaggerates the resultant and may come from adding the two vectors first and then performing another incorrect calculation. The resultant of two perpendicular vectors must always be shorter than their arithmetic sum.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently presents displacement, force and velocity questions involving movements due east and due north. Whenever you notice two vectors acting at right angles, immediately think of a right-angled triangle and apply Pythagoras' theorem.

Also remember this important comparison:

• Same direction → Add the magnitudes.
• Opposite directions → Subtract the magnitudes.
• Perpendicular directions → Use Pythagoras' theorem.

Recognising the relationship between the vectors is often the key to selecting the correct method before doing any calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine standing at one corner of a field.

You walk 24 m towards the east and then 18 m towards the north. Instead of retracing your path, imagine drawing a straight line from where you started directly to where you ended.

That straight line is called the **resultant displacement**.

Because the two movements are at right angles, they form a right-angled triangle.

Using Pythagoras' theorem:

24² = 576

18² = 324

576 + 324 = 900

√900 = 30 m.

So, although the total distance walked is 42 m, the engineer ends up only 30 m away from the starting point in a straight line. That straight-line distance is the resultant displacement.

question: A warehouse manager observes that a heavy generator is being pushed simultaneously by two workers from different directions so that it moves smoothly toward the loading bay. Why is the addition of vectors necessary when analysing the motion of the generator?

options.A: Because the combined effect of both applied forces determines the actual motion of the generator.
options.B: Because every force automatically becomes a scalar when two people apply it.
options.C: Because the masses of the workers must first be added before the generator can move.
options.D: Because forces acting in different directions always cancel each other completely.

answer_text: Because the combined effect of both applied forces determines the actual motion of the generator.

explanation.question_restate: This question examines whether you understand why vectors are added in practical situations involving two or more forces acting simultaneously on the same object.
explanation.principle:
A force is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction. Whenever two or more forces act on the same object at the same time, the object does not respond to each force separately. Instead, it responds to a single force known as the resultant force.

A beginner may ask, 'What does resultant mean?'

The resultant force is the single force that has the same overall effect as all the individual forces acting together.

Another beginner may ask, 'Why can't we simply add the numbers?'

Unlike scalar quantities, vectors have direction. This means that two forces of equal magnitude may strengthen each other, weaken each other or produce motion in an entirely new direction depending on how they are applied.

That is why vector addition is necessary whenever several forces act simultaneously.

In warehouse operations, cranes, towing vehicles, robotics and engineering, the direction of every applied force is just as important as its magnitude.
steps: Step 1: Identify the physical quantity involved. The workers are pushing the generator, so the quantity involved is force.
Step 2: Recall that force is a vector quantity because it has magnitude and direction.
Step 3: Since two forces act on the same object simultaneously, they combine to produce one overall effect called the resultant force.
Step 4: Therefore, vector addition is required to determine the generator's actual motion.
Step 5: Hence, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because a force never becomes a scalar simply because more than one person applies it. Force is always a vector quantity.
Option C is incorrect because the masses of the workers are irrelevant to why vector addition is used. The question concerns the forces they apply, not their masses.
Option D is incorrect because forces acting in different directions do not always cancel completely. Whether they cancel depends on their magnitudes and directions.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB often tests your understanding of why vector addition is required before asking you to perform calculations. Whenever more than one force, velocity or displacement acts simultaneously, immediately think of the resultant vector.
explanation.final_answer: A. Because the combined effect of both applied forces determines the actual motion of the generator.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two friends pushing a broken car. The car does not respond to each person's push separately. Instead, it moves according to the combined push of both people. Physics calls this combined push the resultant force. Finding that combined force is called vector addition.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly explanations, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question: During a laboratory practical, two students investigate the effect of combining forces on a metal ring placed on a smooth horizontal board. One spring balance pulls the ring with a force of 8 N towards the east, while a second spring balance simultaneously pulls it with a force of 6 N towards the north. Which statement best describes the resultant force acting on the ring?

options.A: The resultant force has a magnitude of 10 N and acts in a direction between east and north.
options.B: The resultant force is 14 N because the two forces are simply added together.
options.C: The resultant force is 2 N because the smaller force is subtracted from the larger one.
options.D: The forces cancel each other because they act simultaneously.

answer_text: The resultant force has a magnitude of 10 N and acts in a direction between east and north.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how forces acting at right angles combine to produce a single resultant force during a laboratory experiment. Rather than merely adding the numerical values, you must consider both the magnitudes and the directions of the forces.
explanation.principle:
Before solving the question, let us understand the important ideas involved.

A force is a push or a pull that can change the state of motion of an object. Force is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

A beginner may ask, 'Why is direction important?'

Suppose two people push a table with equal strength but in different directions. The table does not respond as if only one person is pushing. Instead, the combined effect depends on both the sizes of the pushes and the directions in which they act.

When two or more vectors act together, they can be replaced by a single vector known as the **resultant vector**. The resultant produces exactly the same effect as all the individual vectors acting together.

If two vectors act in the same direction, their magnitudes are added directly.

If they act in opposite directions, their magnitudes are subtracted.

However, if they act at right angles (90°), neither simple addition nor subtraction is correct. Instead, the resultant is found using **Pythagoras' theorem**:

R² = x² + y²

where:

• R is the magnitude of the resultant force.
• x and y are the two perpendicular forces.

The direction of the resultant always lies between the directions of the two original vectors.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the two forces.

Force 1 = 8 N towards the east.

Force 2 = 6 N towards the north.
Step 2: Observe that east and north are perpendicular directions. Therefore, the forces meet at an angle of 90°.

Step 3: Apply Pythagoras' theorem.

R² = 8² + 6²

Step 4: Square each force.

8² = 64

6² = 36

Step 5: Add the squares.

64 + 36 = 100

Step 6: Take the square root.

R = √100 = 10 N.

Step 7: Determine the direction.

The resultant force cannot point exactly east or exactly north because both forces contribute to it.

It acts in a direction between east and north.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because forces acting at right angles cannot be added arithmetically. Adding 8 N and 6 N to obtain 14 N ignores the directions of the forces. This is one of the most common mistakes made by candidates.
Option C is incorrect because subtraction is used only when two vectors act along the same straight line but in opposite directions. In this experiment, the forces are perpendicular, not opposite.
Option D is incorrect because perpendicular forces do not cancel one another. Forces cancel only when they have equal magnitudes and act in exactly opposite directions.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB often tests whether candidates know **when to add, subtract or apply Pythagoras' theorem**.

Remember these three rules:

• Same direction → Add the vectors.

• Opposite direction → Subtract the vectors.

• Right-angle directions → Use Pythagoras' theorem.

Before performing any calculation, always examine the directions of the vectors. The correct method depends on the angle between them.
explanation.final_answer: A. The resultant force has a magnitude of 10 N and acts in a direction between east and north.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine tying two ropes to a ring.

One person pulls towards the east with 8 N, while another pulls towards the north with 6 N.

The ring feels both pulls at the same time.

It does not move only east or only north. Instead, it moves in a direction between the two pulls.

Using Pythagoras' theorem:

8² + 6² = 64 + 36 = 100

√100 = 10 N.

So, the two forces combine to produce a single force of 10 N acting between east and north.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed beginner-friendly lessons, practical demonstrations, and additional exam-focused practice.

question: During the installation of a high-voltage transmission line, a steel support mast is held in position by three tension cables attached simultaneously. The first cable exerts a force of 80 N due north, the second exerts a force of 60 N due south, while the third exerts a force of 40 N due north. What is the magnitude and direction of the resultant force acting on the mast due to the three cables?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the single force that has the same overall effect as three forces acting along the same straight line. The skill being tested is multi-stage vector addition, where vectors are added one after another while considering their directions.
explanation.principle:
Before solving the question, let us understand what happens when several vectors act together.

A vector is a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Force is a vector because saying '80 N' is not enough—you must also specify the direction in which the force acts.

When more than two vectors act on an object, they are added one after another. This process is called **multi-stage vector addition**.

A beginner may ask, 'Do I always use Pythagoras' theorem when adding vectors?'

No.

The method depends on the directions of the vectors.

• If all the vectors act along the same straight line, simply assign one direction as positive and the opposite direction as negative, then perform ordinary algebraic addition.

• If the vectors are perpendicular, use Pythagoras' theorem.

• If the vectors act at other angles, methods such as the triangle law, parallelogram law or trigonometry may be required.

In this question, every force acts either due north or due south. Since all the forces lie along one straight line, they can be added algebraically after taking account of their directions.
steps:
Step 1: Choose one direction as positive.

Let north be positive (+).

Therefore:

80 N north = +80 N

60 N south = -60 N

40 N north = +40 N.

Step 2: Add the vectors one after another.

Resultant = (+80) + (-60) + (+40)

Step 3: Perform the calculation.

80 - 60 + 40 = 60 N.

Step 4: Interpret the sign of the answer.

The answer is positive, meaning the resultant force acts towards the north.

Step 5: State the final answer.

The resultant force is **60 N due north**.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. 60 N due north is correct because the northward forces are added together before the southward force is subtracted: 80 + 40 - 60 = 60 N.
B. 100 N due north is obtained by adding only the two northward forces and forgetting to subtract the southward force. This is a common mistake when candidates ignore opposite directions.
C. 20 N due north results from subtracting the forces incorrectly, such as calculating 80 - 60 - 40. The third force actually acts northward and should increase, not decrease, the resultant.
D. 180 N due north is simply the arithmetic sum of all three magnitudes. This ignores the fact that one of the forces acts in the opposite direction.
explanation.exam_tip:
One of JAMB's favourite tricks is to include several vectors acting along the same line. Before calculating, always group vectors according to their directions.

Remember this simple rule:

• Same direction → Add.
• Opposite direction → Subtract.

Do not automatically use Pythagoras' theorem unless the vectors are perpendicular.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine three people pulling a pole.

Two people pull towards the north with forces of 80 N and 40 N. Together they pull with 120 N.

Another person pulls towards the south with 60 N.

Since the pulls are in opposite directions, the smaller pull reduces the larger one.

120 N - 60 N = 60 N.

Therefore, the pole experiences a resultant force of **60 N towards the north**.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, worked examples and additional exam-focused practice.

question:
During a practical lesson, students discussed the addition of two force vectors acting simultaneously on a steel ring. Four students made the following statements:

Student I: 'The resultant of two vectors is always equal to the arithmetic sum of their magnitudes.'

Student II: 'The direction of each vector must be considered when adding vectors.'

Student III: 'Two vectors of equal magnitude always produce a resultant equal to twice either vector.'

Student IV: 'The magnitude of the resultant depends on both the magnitudes of the vectors and the angle between them.'

Which combination of students made correct statements?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your conceptual understanding of vector addition by asking you to identify common misconceptions. Rather than performing calculations, you must determine which statements correctly describe how vectors are added.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, it is important to understand what a vector is.

A vector is a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Magnitude tells us how large the quantity is, while direction tells us where it acts.

Examples of vector quantities include force, displacement, velocity, acceleration and momentum.

When two or more vectors act together, they can be replaced by a single vector called the **resultant vector**. The resultant produces exactly the same overall effect as all the individual vectors acting together.

A beginner may ask, 'Why can't we simply add the numbers?'

Unlike ordinary numbers, vectors possess direction. Two forces of 10 N may reinforce each other if they act in the same direction, partially cancel if they act at an angle, or cancel completely if they act in opposite directions.

Therefore, vector addition depends on:

• the magnitude of each vector; and
• the angle between the vectors.

This is one of the most important ideas in elementary vector physics and is frequently tested by JAMB through conceptual questions.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Student I's statement.

'The resultant of two vectors is always equal to the arithmetic sum of their magnitudes.'

This statement is incorrect.

Simple arithmetic addition is valid only when the vectors act in exactly the same direction. If they act at an angle or in opposite directions, the resultant is different.

Step 2: Examine Student II's statement.

'The direction of each vector must be considered when adding vectors.'

This statement is correct.

Direction is one of the defining properties of a vector. Ignoring direction leads to incorrect results.

Step 3: Examine Student III's statement.

'Two vectors of equal magnitude always produce a resultant equal to twice either vector.'

This statement is incorrect.

If two equal vectors act in the same direction, the resultant is indeed twice one vector.

However, if they act in opposite directions, the resultant is zero.

If they act at right angles, the resultant is √2 times one vector.

Therefore, the word 'always' makes the statement false.

Step 4: Examine Student IV's statement.

'The magnitude of the resultant depends on both the magnitudes of the vectors and the angle between them.'

This statement is correct.

The angle between vectors is an essential factor in determining the magnitude and direction of the resultant vector.

Step 5: Identify all correct statements.

Correct statements are those made by Student II and Student IV.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (I and III only) is incorrect because both statements contain the word 'always', making them scientifically false. Vector addition depends on direction and the angle between vectors.
Option C (I, II and IV only) is incorrect because Student I wrongly assumes vectors can always be added arithmetically without considering direction.
Option D (II, III and IV only) is incorrect because Student III ignores the effect of the angle between vectors. Equal vectors do not always produce the same resultant.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently includes words such as 'always', 'never', 'only' and 'must' in conceptual Physics questions. These words often signal statements that should be examined carefully.

For vector addition, remember this golden rule:

**The magnitude of the resultant is determined not only by the sizes of the vectors but also by the angle between them.**

Never assume that vectors can be treated like ordinary numbers unless they act along exactly the same line and in the same direction.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of two people pushing a stalled vehicle.

If both push in exactly the same direction, their efforts add together.

If one pushes from the side, part of the force changes the direction of motion instead of increasing the forward push.

If they push in opposite directions with equal force, the vehicle may not move at all.

This shows that adding vectors is different from adding ordinary numbers. The direction in which each vector acts is just as important as its size.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and extensive exam-focused practice.

question:
During a practical lesson on vector addition, four students made the following statements:

Student I: 'The resultant of two vectors is always equal to the arithmetic sum of their magnitudes.'

Student II: 'The resultant vector represents the single vector that has the same overall effect as all the vectors acting together.'

Student III: 'When adding vectors, both magnitude and direction must be considered.'

Student IV: 'Graphical methods such as the head-to-tail and parallelogram methods can be used to add vectors.'

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question is a comprehensive review of vector addition. Instead of performing calculations, it tests your understanding of the fundamental principles governing how vectors are added and interpreted.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, it is important to understand what vector addition means.

A vector is a physical quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction. Examples include force, displacement, velocity, acceleration and momentum.

A beginner may ask, 'What does it mean to add vectors?'

Vector addition is the process of combining two or more vectors to produce another vector called the **resultant vector**.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is a resultant vector?'

The resultant vector is the single vector that can replace all the individual vectors while producing exactly the same overall effect.

Unlike ordinary numbers, vectors cannot always be added by simply adding their magnitudes because direction influences the final result.

For example:

• Two forces acting in exactly the same direction reinforce each other, so their magnitudes are added.

• Two forces acting in opposite directions oppose each other, so their magnitudes are subtracted.

• Two vectors acting at an angle require graphical methods or mathematical techniques such as the triangle law, parallelogram law or vector resolution.

Therefore, successful vector addition always considers both magnitude and direction.

Common graphical methods include:

• Head-to-tail method.
• Triangle method.
• Parallelogram method.

These methods all produce the same resultant when applied correctly.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Student I's statement.

'The resultant of two vectors is always equal to the arithmetic sum of their magnitudes.'

This statement is incorrect.

Vectors are influenced by direction. The arithmetic sum is obtained only when the vectors act in exactly the same direction. In many situations, such as perpendicular or opposite vectors, the resultant is different.

Step 2: Examine Student II's statement.

This statement correctly defines a resultant vector as the single vector that has the same overall effect as all the individual vectors combined.

Therefore, Student II is correct.

Step 3: Examine Student III's statement.

This statement is also correct because vector addition requires consideration of both magnitude and direction.

Ignoring direction is one of the most common mistakes students make.

Step 4: Examine Student IV's statement.

This statement is correct.

The head-to-tail method and the parallelogram method are both recognised graphical techniques for adding vectors.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Student I is incorrect.

Students II, III and IV are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Student I. The arithmetic sum of magnitudes is not always the resultant. Direction must always be considered.
Option C is incorrect because Student I is false, while Student II and Student III, which are correct, have been omitted.
Option D is incorrect because although Students III and IV are correct, Student I is still incorrect. Including one incorrect statement makes the entire combination wrong.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB does not always test vector addition through calculations. Many questions assess your conceptual understanding.

Memorise these key ideas:

• Scalars are added algebraically.
• Vectors are added by considering both magnitude and direction.
• The resultant is the single vector that produces the same effect as all the original vectors combined.
• Graphical methods include the head-to-tail, triangle and parallelogram methods.

Whenever you see the word 'always' in a physics statement, examine it carefully. Absolute statements are frequently used by JAMB to test conceptual understanding.
explanation.final_answer: A. II, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine several people pushing a stalled vehicle.

Instead of calculating the effect of each person's push separately, you can replace all the pushes with one imaginary push that has exactly the same effect. That single push is called the resultant force.

To find this resultant, you must consider not only how hard each person pushes but also the direction in which they push.

This is why Student II, Student III and Student IV are correct.

Student I is wrong because vectors cannot always be added like ordinary numbers.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries, and additional exam-focused practice.

question: An autonomous warehouse robot is programmed to move 18 m due east to deliver a package. During operation, a correction command instructs it to move 7 m due west before continuing. Which of the following best represents the robot's net displacement from its starting point?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of vector subtraction using a practical warehouse automation scenario. It requires you to determine the robot's net displacement after moving in opposite directions.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Examples include displacement, velocity, force and acceleration.

Vector subtraction occurs when one vector acts in the opposite direction to another. Instead of simply adding magnitudes, the directions must first be compared.

When two vectors act along the same straight line but in opposite directions, the smaller magnitude is subtracted from the larger magnitude. The resultant takes the direction of the larger vector.

Mathematically,

Resultant = Larger vector − Smaller vector

provided the vectors are collinear and point in opposite directions.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the two displacement vectors.

Initial displacement = 18 m due east.

Correction displacement = 7 m due west.

Step 2: Observe that the vectors act in opposite directions.

Since east and west are opposite directions, the vectors oppose each other.

Step 3: Subtract the smaller magnitude from the larger magnitude.

Resultant displacement = 18 m − 7 m = 11 m.

Step 4: Determine the direction.

Because the eastward displacement (18 m) is greater than the westward displacement (7 m), the resultant points due east.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The correct answer is 11 m due east, which corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it adds the two magnitudes. Addition is used only when the vectors act in the same direction.
Option C is incorrect because although the magnitude is obtained by subtraction, the resultant cannot point west since the eastward displacement is larger.
Option D is incorrect because it both adds the magnitudes and assigns the wrong direction.
explanation.common_misconception: A common mistake is to add the magnitudes whenever two vectors are mentioned. In vector subtraction, direction is just as important as magnitude. Opposite directions require subtraction, not addition.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, whenever two vectors lie on the same straight line:

• Same direction → Add the magnitudes.
• Opposite directions → Subtract the magnitudes.
• The resultant always points in the direction of the larger vector.

Always identify the directions before performing any calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine taking 18 steps forward and then 7 steps backward. You have effectively moved only 11 steps forward from where you started. The same idea applies to vector subtraction because the backward movement cancels part of the forward movement.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Subtraction of Vectors for a complete revision of vector subtraction, opposite-direction vectors, sign convention, resultant displacement, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries, and additional exam-focused practice.

question: An autonomous warehouse robot first moves 18 m due east to deliver a package. It then moves 10 m due west to return towards its charging station. Which vector represents the robot's net displacement from its starting point?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of vector subtraction using motion along the same straight line but in opposite directions. You are required to determine the robot's net displacement after moving east and then west.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Displacement is a vector because it describes both how far an object moves and the direction of that movement.

When two vectors act along the same straight line but in opposite directions, they are combined by subtracting their magnitudes. The direction of the larger vector becomes the direction of the resultant vector.

A beginner may ask, 'Why do we subtract instead of add?'

The second movement opposes the first movement. Since the robot moves back towards its starting point, part of its initial displacement is cancelled. Therefore, only the difference between the two distances remains.

Rule:

• Same direction → add the magnitudes.
• Opposite directions → subtract the magnitudes.
• The resultant points in the direction of the larger vector.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the first vector.

The robot moves 18 m due east.

Step 2: Identify the second vector.

The robot then moves 10 m due west. Since west is opposite to east, this vector opposes the first one.

Step 3: Perform vector subtraction.

Resultant displacement = 18 m − 10 m = 8 m.

Step 4: Determine the direction.

The larger displacement is 18 m due east, so the resultant also points due east.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The correct vector is 8 m due east, which corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because although the magnitude is 8 m, the resultant cannot point west since the eastward displacement is greater than the westward displacement.
Option C is incorrect because it adds the magnitudes instead of subtracting them. Addition is only appropriate when both vectors act in the same direction.
Option D is incorrect because it both adds the magnitudes incorrectly and assigns the wrong direction.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever two vectors act along the same straight line in opposite directions, think of one vector cancelling part of the other. Subtract their magnitudes and assign the direction of the larger vector. JAMB frequently tests this basic principle before introducing more advanced vector problems.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine walking 18 m forward and then walking back 10 m. You have not travelled a total of 28 m away from where you started. Instead, you are only 8 m from your starting point in the forward direction. The same idea applies to vector subtraction.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Subtraction of Vectors for a complete revision of opposite-direction vectors, signed vector representation, displacement problems, force subtraction, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries, and additional exam-focused practice.

question: An autonomous warehouse robot first moves 18 m due east to deliver a package. It is then required to return towards its charging station by moving 7 m due west. Which of the following correctly represents the robot's net displacement after these two movements?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of vector subtraction using a practical warehouse robotics scenario. You are required to determine the resultant displacement when two vectors act in opposite directions.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Displacement is a vector because it describes not only how far an object moves but also the direction of movement.

Vector subtraction occurs naturally whenever one vector acts in the opposite direction to another. Instead of simply adding the distances travelled, the directions must first be considered.

For motion along the same straight line:

• Vectors in the same direction are added.
• Vectors in opposite directions are subtracted.
• The direction of the larger vector becomes the direction of the resultant.

A beginner may ask, 'Why do we subtract instead of add?'

The westward movement partially cancels the earlier eastward movement. Therefore, only the difference between the two magnitudes remains as the net displacement.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the first displacement.

The robot moves 18 m due east.

Step 2: Identify the second displacement.

The robot then moves 7 m due west, which is opposite to the first displacement.

Step 3: Apply the rule for opposite vectors.

Resultant displacement = 18 m − 7 m = 11 m.

Step 4: Determine the direction.

Since the eastward displacement (18 m) is greater than the westward displacement (7 m), the resultant points due east.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The correct answer is 11 m due east.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it adds the magnitudes (18 + 7 = 25 m), ignoring that the vectors act in opposite directions.
Option C is incorrect because although the magnitude is obtained by subtraction, it assigns the direction incorrectly. The larger displacement is towards the east.
Option D is incorrect because it both adds the magnitudes and assigns the wrong direction.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever two vectors lie along the same straight line, check their directions before performing any calculation. If they point in opposite directions, subtract their magnitudes and assign the direction of the larger vector. Many JAMB candidates lose marks by adding magnitudes without considering direction.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine walking 18 steps forward and then 7 steps backward. You have not travelled 25 steps away from your starting point. Instead, the backward movement cancels part of the forward movement, leaving you 11 steps in front of where you started. The same idea applies to vector subtraction.

question: An autonomous warehouse robot moves 18 m due east to collect a package. It then needs to determine the vector that must be subtracted from its first displacement so that the resulting displacement is 12 m due east. Which vector should be subtracted?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of vector subtraction. You are required to determine the vector that should be removed from an initial displacement of 18 m east to leave a resultant displacement of 12 m east.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction. Examples include displacement, velocity, force and acceleration.

Vector subtraction means removing the effect of one vector from another.

Mathematically,

A − B = Resultant

where:
• A is the original vector.
• B is the vector being subtracted.
• The resultant is the remaining vector after subtraction.

Unlike ordinary numbers, vectors must always be treated with their directions. When vectors act along the same straight line, east may be taken as positive while west is taken as negative.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Original displacement = 18 m east.

Required resultant displacement = 12 m east.

Step 2: Write the vector equation.

Original vector − Unknown vector = Resultant vector.

18 m east − X = 12 m east.

Step 3: Solve for the unknown vector.

X = 18 m east − 12 m east.

X = 6 m east.

Step 4: Verify the result.

Subtracting 6 m east from 18 m east leaves 12 m east.

Therefore, the required vector is 6 m due east.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because subtracting a westward vector is equivalent to adding an eastward vector, giving 24 m east instead of 12 m east.
Option C is incorrect because subtracting 30 m east from 18 m east gives 12 m west, not 12 m east.
Option D is incorrect because subtracting 30 m west is equivalent to adding 30 m east, giving 48 m east.
explanation.exam_tip: When vectors lie along the same straight line, first assign opposite directions opposite signs. Write the subtraction as an equation before solving. Always verify that the direction of the final vector agrees with the question.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine you have travelled 18 m east but want your final journey to be only 12 m east. You simply remove 6 m of the eastward movement. Therefore, the vector that must be subtracted is 6 m due east.

question: During a rescue operation, an emergency response boat travels 18 km due north from the base camp to reach a flooded village. It then returns by travelling 11 km due south to deliver supplies to another community. Which vector represents the boat's displacement from the base camp after the second journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of vector subtraction by determining the final displacement after two journeys made in opposite directions.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Displacement is a vector because it describes not only how far an object moves but also the direction of the movement.

When two vectors act in opposite directions along the same straight line, vector subtraction is used. Instead of adding the magnitudes directly, the smaller magnitude is subtracted from the larger one, and the resultant takes the direction of the larger vector.

This principle is commonly applied in navigation, transportation, rescue operations and motion along a straight path.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the first displacement.

The boat travels 18 km due north.

Represent this as +18 km.

Step 2: Identify the second displacement.

The boat then travels 11 km due south.

Since south is opposite to north, represent this as −11 km.

Step 3: Perform the vector subtraction.

Resultant displacement = 18 km − 11 km = 7 km.

Step 4: Determine the direction.

The larger displacement is towards the north, so the resultant displacement is 7 km due north.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option A correctly gives 7 km due north.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the final displacement cannot be south. The initial northward journey is greater than the southward return.
Option C is incorrect because it adds the two distances instead of subtracting opposite vectors. This gives the total distance travelled, not the displacement.
Option D is incorrect because it both adds the distances incorrectly and assigns the wrong direction.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever two vectors act in opposite directions along the same straight line, subtract their magnitudes and assign the direction of the larger vector. Remember that JAMB often tests the difference between total distance travelled and resultant displacement.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine walking 18 km north and then coming back 11 km south. You have not returned to where you started. You are still 7 km north of your starting point. Therefore, your displacement is 7 km due north.

question: An autonomous delivery drone flies 26 km due north from its charging station to Location P. It is later instructed to fly directly to Location Q, which is 9 km due north of the same charging station. Which vector correctly represents the drone's displacement from Location P to Location Q?

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of vector subtraction by determining the displacement from one position to another using their position vectors measured from the same reference point.
explanation.principle:
A vector has both magnitude and direction. When two locations are described relative to the same origin, the displacement from one location to the other is obtained by subtracting their position vectors.

If the initial position vector is A and the final position vector is B, then:

Displacement = B − A

The sign of the result indicates direction. A negative value means the displacement is opposite to the chosen positive direction.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the position vectors.

Location P = 26 km due north.

Location Q = 9 km due north.

Step 2: Apply vector subtraction.

Displacement from P to Q = Q − P

= 9 km north − 26 km north

= −17 km north.

Step 3: Interpret the negative sign.

A negative northward displacement means the drone must travel 17 km due south.

Step 4: Compare with the options.

Therefore, the correct answer is 17 km due south.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it ignores the change in direction after subtraction.
Option C is incorrect because it adds the two position vectors instead of subtracting them.
Option D is incorrect because the direction is correct but the magnitude results from incorrect addition.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB questions, when two positions are measured from the same reference point, always subtract the initial position vector from the final position vector. After obtaining the numerical answer, interpret its sign before deciding the direction.
explanation.simple_explanation: The drone first reaches a point 26 km north of its station. Since the destination is only 9 km north of the station, the drone has gone too far and must return. The difference is 17 km, so it flies 17 km due south.
